---
title: "AI and Copyright"
subtitle: "Security, Privacy, and Consumer Protection"
author: "Nick Feamster · University of Chicago"
date: last-modified
---

## From the Four Factors to the Training Set {.center}

> Last time: copyright protects **expression**, and **fair use** is a four-factor
> defense that turns on **transformativeness**.

Today we point that exact framework at the input to every modern AI model:
**billions of copyrighted works, copied without permission, to train a model.**

::: {.notes}
This deck is the sequel to 14-Copyright. Assume students know the idea/expression line,
the four-factor test, and Google v. Oracle. Don't re-teach them — apply them. The single
organizing question all day: is copying-to-train fair use, and does it matter how you got
the data and what the model spits back out? Per agenda, the for/against AI case-study
drill is a near-certain midterm item, so make them argue both sides.
:::

## A 2026 Vignette: The Law Is Splitting in Real Time {.smaller}

::: {.vignette}
In **2025–26** the first AI-training rulings landed — and they **disagree**.
**Feb 11, 2025**, *Thomson Reuters v. Ross* (D. Del.): training a legal-research AI on
Westlaw headnotes was **not** fair use. **June 23, 2025**, *Bartz v. Anthropic* (N.D.
Cal.): training on books **is** "exceedingly transformative" — but pirating 7M+ books to
build the library was **not**; Anthropic later settled for **~$1.5B** (~$3,000/work).
Meanwhile *NYT v. OpenAI* survived dismissal and, as of early 2026, is fighting over
**"regurgitation."**
:::

Same statute, opposite results — the **facts** (how acquired, how transformative, how
much it competes) decide everything.

::: {.notes}
Three verified, dated facts. The teaching point: there is no "AI fair use rule" yet —
outcomes diverge on the facts. Ross lost because its tool directly competed with Westlaw
(non-transformative, commercial). Anthropic won on training but lost on piracy. NYT is
still live. Don't claim the Supreme Court has ruled — it hasn't. This is the freshest
hook; swap in any newer ruling at lecture time.
:::

## How Models "Learn": Copying Is Step Zero {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Traditional code**

Explicit rules a human writes:
"if input is X, output Y."
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Machine learning**

1. **Copy** millions of examples
2. Model finds statistical patterns
3. Generate new outputs from patterns
:::
:::

The legally load-bearing fact: **training requires making copies** of the data — full
works, ingested into the pipeline. That copy is what plaintiffs sue over.

::: {.notes}
For non-CS students: traditional programming is a recipe; ML is learning to cook by
tasting thousands of dishes. The point for this course is narrow and crucial — to learn
the patterns, the system must first *reproduce* the works into the training pipeline.
Whether that intermediate copy is fair use is the whole fight. Connect to the memorization
risk introduced in the AI-Privacy lecture.
:::

## What Is in the Training Data? {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Text models** (GPT, Claude, Gemini)

- Books — incl. **Books3** (~196k titles, scraped from a pirate library)
- News, journalism, blogs
- **Common Crawl** (billions of web pages)
- Code repos, scientific papers
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Image / multimodal models**

- **LAION-5B** (~5.85B image–text pairs)
- Stock photos, artist portfolios
- Social-media images
:::
:::

The scale dwarfs prior cases: Oracle was **11,500 lines of code**; a frontier model
trains on a meaningful fraction of the **entire public internet**.

::: {.notes}
Anchor scale against Oracle from last lecture: 11,500 lines vs. ~the whole web. Books3
is the "smoking gun" plaintiffs lean on — it came from a pirate site (Bibliotik), so
training on it bundles a piracy problem with the fair-use problem (exactly the Anthropic
split). Note Books3 was pulled offline in 2023, but models trained on it persist.
:::

## Does Scale Change the Fair-Use Answer? {.smaller}

Run the **four factors** against AI training (you already know the test):

| Factor | AI companies say | Rights holders say |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Purpose** | Transformative — learn patterns, new tool | Commercial substitution, supersedes |
| **2. Nature** | Lots of factual/functional data | Novels, art, lyrics = highly creative |
| **3. Amount** | Whole work needed; tiny per-work effect | Wholesale copying, no licensing tried |
| **4. Market** | Different market; may grow demand | Lost sales + lost **AI-licensing** market |

**The crux:** does the *purpose of the copy* (training) control, or the *purpose of the
output* (which may substitute)?

::: {.notes}
This is the slide to spend time on — it is the midterm drill in table form. Have them
argue each cell both ways. Factor 1 is doing most of the work in the rulings (Anthropic,
Meta leaned transformative; Ross leaned commercial-competition). Factor 4 is the authors'
strongest card and the angle the Anthropic court flagged as potentially most persuasive:
a model can *flood the market* with substitutes.
:::

## The Lawsuit Landscape {.smaller}

| Case | Defendant | What's at stake |
|---|---|---|
| **NYT v. OpenAI** (Dec 2023) | OpenAI / Microsoft | News; verbatim **regurgitation**, paywall bypass |
| **Authors Guild v. OpenAI** (Sep 2023) | OpenAI | Novels (Grisham, Martin, Picoult…) |
| **Kadrey / Silverman v. Meta** | Meta (LLaMA) | Books3; **Meta won** training ruling (Jun 2025) |
| **Bartz v. Anthropic** | Anthropic | Books; **split** ruling + ~$1.5B settlement |
| **Getty v. Stability AI** (Feb 2023) | Stability AI | 12M images; **watermarks** in outputs |
| **Thomson Reuters v. Ross** | Ross | Westlaw headnotes; **not** fair use |

The plaintiffs span **every creative sector** — text, news, images, legal content.

::: {.notes}
Don't read the whole table — pick two contrasts. (1) Anthropic vs. Ross: same year, both
N.D. Cal./D. Del., opposite outcomes, because Ross built a *direct competitor* to the
source. (2) Getty's evidence is uniquely vivid: generated images carried a garbled "Getty
Images" watermark — near-direct proof the training set was copied. The Meta and Anthropic
training wins are district-court, not appellate; cases are consolidating and several will
likely head up on appeal.
:::

## NYT v. OpenAI: The Regurgitation Fight {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**NYT's evidence**

- ChatGPT reproduced article passages **near-verbatim**
- Output paywalled content
- Substitutes for a subscription
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**OpenAI's defense**

- Training is **transformative** fair use
- Regurgitation is a rare **bug**, induced by adversarial prompts
- Model weights aren't "copies"
:::
:::

**Status (early 2026):** survived a motion to dismiss (Mar 2025); core infringement
claims proceed. The live question is whether **memorization** defeats the fair-use story.

::: {.notes}
NYT was one of the few major publishers that *refused* to license to OpenAI — meanwhile
OpenAI signed AP, Axel Springer, the FT. That refusal is why this is the marquee case.
Judge Stein narrowed but did not dismiss. The reason this case matters more than the
others: it foregrounds *output* memorization, which is the AI companies' biggest
vulnerability (next slides). Demo idea: ask a current chatbot for a news passage and show
it now refuses — that refusal is post-litigation mitigation, not innocence or guilt.
:::

## The Memorization Problem {.smaller}

**Memorization** = the model can reproduce training data **verbatim**, not just learn
from it.

- More likely for **duplicated** or **rare/distinctive** text; **larger models**
  memorize more (Carlini et al., extracting training data from LLMs)
- Demonstrated across text, code, images, and song lyrics

**Why it's legally lethal:**

- Undercuts the **"we only learn patterns"** transformative-use claim
- Looks like **storage + retrieval**, not abstraction
- Verbatim output is **direct market substitution** — and a *separate* infringement

::: {.notes}
This is the through-line from the AI-Privacy lecture (memorization of training data) —
now the same phenomenon is the copyright plaintiffs' best weapon. Contrast with Google
Books, which was *designed* to never show the full text (snippets only); a generative
model *can* emit full passages, even if rarely. The open legal question: is *any*
memorization fatal, or is de minimis/adversarially-induced regurgitation excused? That is
exactly what NYT v. OpenAI is testing.
:::

## The "AI Learns Like a Human" Analogy {.smaller}

**AI companies:** "Artists study other artists; writers read to learn. AI does the same —
copyright never barred *learning*."

**The rebuttal, factor by factor:**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Scale:** hundreds of books vs. **billions** of works
- **Memory:** human imperfect recall vs. **verbatim** reproduction
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Purpose:** personal growth vs. a **commercial product**
- **Output:** one person's limited output vs. **infinite, zero-marginal-cost** generation
:::
:::

**Discussion:** is the analogy persuasive — or does it paper over differences that the law
should care about?

::: {.notes}
Great cold-call slide. Steel-man both sides. The strongest pro-analogy point: copyright
genuinely does *not* protect ideas or the act of learning, only expression — so maybe the
right thing to regulate is *outputs*, not training. The strongest anti point: a human who
memorizes and re-sells a book *is* infringing; scale + verbatim memory makes AI more like
that than like a reader. Tie back to the 2025 split: courts are increasingly distinguishing
how-it-learned (often OK) from what-it-outputs (must show substantial similarity).
:::

## The Licensing-Market Question {.smaller}

Factor 4's hardest sub-question: **is there a market for licensing to AI — and did the
training destroy it?**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Rights holders**

- A licensing market **exists**: OpenAI pays AP, Axel Springer, the FT, Reddit
- Payment **proves** the value they took
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**AI companies**

- The market didn't exist *when training occurred*
- Per *Campbell*, holders can't **preempt a transformative market**
:::
:::

**The new front (2026):** courts increasingly ask plaintiffs to prove a **specific output
is substantially similar** to their work — shifting the fight from *input* to *output*.

::: {.notes}
Campbell v. Acuff-Rose: "copyright holders may not preempt the exploitation of
transformative markets." AI companies use this; rights holders counter that the flood of
deals (AP, FT, Reddit, etc.) shows a real, takeable market. The 2026 doctrinal shift is
worth flagging as the freshest analytic move: even where training is deemed fair use,
plaintiffs may still win on *outputs* that mirror specific works. That is where the next
wave of litigation is heading.
:::

## Who Owns the *Output*? {.smaller}

Flip side of the same coin: if a human prompts an AI, **who is the author?**

::: {.vignette}
**U.S. Copyright Office report (Jan 29, 2025):** human authorship is a "bedrock" —
**purely AI-generated output is not copyrightable**, and **prompts alone don't make you
the author**. But human **selection, arrangement, and modification** of AI output *can*
be protected, case-by-case. Existing law is "adequate" — no new statute needed.
:::

So: you can't copyright what the model made for you, but you *can* copyright the creative
human work you wrap around it.

::: {.notes}
This is the ownership half of the lecture title and a common student question ("if I make
an AI image, do I own it?"). Answer: not the raw output; possibly your creative additions.
This is the same human-authorship line from the older Thaler "monkey selfie"-style
reasoning, now formalized for generative AI. Tie it forward: this matters for whether
AI-assisted student/portfolio work is protectable, and for downstream liability if an
output infringes.
:::

## International Approaches Diverge {.smaller}

| Jurisdiction | Stance on training |
|---|---|
| **Japan** | **Permissive** — Art. 30-4 (2018) allows copying for "information analysis," even commercial |
| **United States** | **Uncertain** — courts deciding case-by-case; no statute |
| **European Union** | **Restrictive-ish** — TDM exception **with opt-out** + AI Act training-data **transparency** |
| **United Kingdom** | **In flux** — broad TDM-for-AI proposal stalled after creative-industry pushback |

**Consequence:** jurisdiction shopping — where you train can change whether it's legal.

::: {.notes}
The spread is the lesson: Japan = innovation-first safe harbor; EU = transparency +
rights-reservation (the AI Act requires a "sufficiently detailed summary" of training
data and honors copyright opt-outs); UK keeps reversing course; US punts to courts. This
fragmentation creates real forum/jurisdiction-shopping incentives and is why an "AI
copyright" question is also an international-policy question. Keep specifics general where
unsure rather than over-asserting dates.
:::

## Policy Levers on the Table {.smaller}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Consent defaults**

- **Opt-out** (AI cos.): default-yes, à la `robots.txt`
- **Opt-in** (creators): default-no, explicit permission
- Compromise: opt-out for old data, opt-in for new
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Compensation & tech**

- **Collective licensing** (ASCAP/BMI model)
- **Compulsory licensing** (set rates, can't block)
- **Watermarking**, **data "nutrition labels"**, do-not-train registries
:::
:::

Every option faces the same hard problems: **valuation, attribution, retroactivity,
enforcement.**

::: {.notes}
The deep tension: requiring opt-in protects creators but may make frontier training
infeasible at scale (and favors incumbents who can afford licenses); opt-out maximizes
data but offloads the burden onto creators. Watermarking has a retroactivity problem
(existing works aren't marked) and an arms-race problem (marks get stripped). Mention
UChicago's own Glaze/Nightshade as creator-side technical self-defense — good local hook.
:::

## Activity: Argue the Case Both Ways {.smaller}

**Scenario.** A startup trains an AI on **50,000 copyrighted novels** to build a
"chapter-by-chapter summary generator," sold by subscription.

**Your task — work the four factors, then take a side:**

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
1. Purpose / character?
2. Nature of the works?
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
3. Amount used?
4. Market effect?
:::
:::

**Pair-share, 5 min** — then I'll cold-call **one pair to argue fair use and one to argue
infringement.**

::: {.notes}
This is the agenda's flagged midterm format — the professor "put real money" on a
for/against AI fair-use case study. Expected reads: (1) commercial + arguably non-
transformative (summaries serve the same informational purpose) — leans against; (2)
novels are highly creative — against; (3) entire books — against; (4) summaries plausibly
substitute for buying — against. Likely *not* fair use — a useful contrast to Anthropic,
where the *training* was transformative but the summary-tool purpose here is closer to
substitution (and to Ross's competing-product problem).
:::

## What Comes Next {.smaller}

- **More splits, then appeals** — expect a circuit split and an eventual Supreme Court
  case (years out)
- **Output-side litigation** grows: prove a specific output is **substantially similar**
- **Licensing markets mature** — more publisher/platform deals
- **Open questions:** liability for infringing outputs (user vs. company?), open-source
  models, training on **synthetic** (AI-generated) data

::: {.notes}
Keep predictions humble — tech outruns courts. The most important trend to leave students
with is the input→output shift: even if training-as-fair-use solidifies, plaintiffs are
pivoting to "your model produced *my* work." Liability allocation (is it the prompting
user or the model provider who infringes?) is genuinely unsettled and is the next exam-
ready frontier.
:::

## Key Takeaways

1. AI training **copies copyrighted works at unprecedented scale** — the four-factor test
   still governs
2. **Transformativeness (Factor 1)** drives the wins; **market harm + memorization** drive
   the losses
3. **2025–26 rulings split:** Anthropic (training fair use), Ross (not), NYT (live)
4. **How you got the data matters** as much as what you do with it (piracy ≠ fair use)
5. **AI-only output isn't copyrightable** — human authorship is required
6. Policy levers (opt-in/out, licensing, watermarks) all hit **valuation + enforcement**

::: {.notes}
Close on the same idea that opened: the law isn't new, the scale is. The four factors you
drilled in 14-Copyright are exactly the tools courts are using. For the midterm: be ready
to take an AI scenario and argue both fair use and infringement, citing specific factors
and the 2025 cases by name.
:::

# Questions? {.center}

The law is being written **right now** — and your generation will finish writing it.
